spec_match.rationale: Models the polling baseline that the spec explicitly cites as the problem (500 ms interval, readText loop) against the new event.

interactive.rationale: Live race with running counters, animated bar chart, and copy-from-anywhere prompt. Clear visual proof of the cost gap.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Promises a side-by-side race with a wasted-read counter; delivers exactly that.

edge_cases.rationale: When `clipboardchange` is missing, the right side falls back to a 500ms poll — but the page doesn't visually flag that it's no longer measuring the event. The fallback silently inflates the event-side counter.

design_system.rationale: Variables only, no raw hex, the .bar elements use accent colours from the design system.

openQuestions.title: Visually flag fallback when event missing
openQuestions.detail: If the page is loaded in a browser without `clipboardchange`, the right-hand column silently becomes a polling column. Either disable the right column with a banner, or recolour its bars to make clear the visitor is no longer seeing event-driven data. As written, an unaware visitor concludes the gap doesn't exist.

openQuestions.title: Add a CPU / battery proxy
openQuestions.detail: The page conveys 'wasted reads' but not what they cost. Add a small derived metric — wall-clock time spent inside readText() — so the proof transcends 'feels wasteful'. devtools.timeline-style.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate poll race condition
openQuestions.detail: A poll at t=0 reads the old value, the user copies at t=10ms, the next poll at t=500ms catches it. That 500ms window is invisible right now — surface it with a 'delay between copy and detection' histogram so visitors see the latency cost on top of the read cost.

summary: Compelling head-to-head and the bars make the cost gap obvious. The fallback-when-missing case is the soft spot — currently the page lies about what it's measuring on unsupported browsers. Fix that and surface latency as well as count.
